data.title: Alphabet Stock Is Flat... But Not For Long
data.description:
Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) (NASDAQ:GOOG), like many of the “Magnificent 7” stocks, has had an oversized position in my portfolio for more than a decade. In my previous Alphabet analysis, I presented my findings, which underscored why I thought the stock is a “Strong Buy,” and pointed out the catalysts that resonated most - with me. That said, the stock is rather flat since that analysis, having underperformed the market by ~4%.

However, Alphabet has been making headlines once again, with both catalysts and risks to their model. I filtered out the noise to (hopefully) add a little clarity to the company’s current situation, and whether or not it's a company worth investing in.

Spoiler: I’m reaffirming my “Strong Buy” rating on GOOGL stock. Here’s why.

Recent News About Alphabet

First, let’s take a quick step back.

Since ChatGPT was released, Alphabet and OpenAI have been fierce rivals. Alphabet’s LLM (Bard) was the first real challenge to ChatGPT. Bard was later rebranded as Gemini. Not only that, ChatGPT was said to be smarter and more powerful, especially when OpenAI announced that GPT-4 would be 100x faster than GPT-3 back in 2023.

The result? ChatGPT has always been considered a superior AI chatbot over Bard - perhaps because of its first-mover advantage.

The media was already impressed with GPT-3 back then, and ChatGPT’s user base started to grow, exponentially. There was also speculation that the SEO industry would drastically change as users will stop using Google, and instead, use AI chatbots for of their internet searches.

Ask yourself for a moment: What would happen to Google if no one used their platform for search?

As an investor with a 9% position, I’d say that’s a rather scary thought. Naturally, if we all started using ChatGPT instead of Google for search, (Alphabet’s) advertisement revenue would plummet.

Here’s the thing, though: getting people to try a new product (or service) is hard, but getting people to change their ways is even harder.

Let me give you an example that, I hope, can resonate. Please bear with me.

In your kitchen, you most likely have paper towels close to your sink. Where do you buy your paper towels from? I would bet your supermarket or some department-like store. If I’m right, ask yourself, why not buy it on Amazon (AMZN)? They invented “1-Click” shopping, removing pretty much all the friction.

My point?

Amazon may offer a more efficient way to buy paper towels, but, buying them from the supermarket is what we’ve always done - even if it's inconvenient.

ChatGPT may offer answers that compete with Google, but that doesn’t mean I’ll change how I search.

Also, while the use of ChatGPT is increasing, Google’s ad revenue hasn’t declined to a point where it should concern investors, and I’ll cover more on that a little later.

Keep Your Friends Close, And Enemies Closer

Naturally, OpenAI and Alphabet are fierce competitors.

However, according to an exclusive report, OpenAI plans to add Alphabet’s Google Cloud services to meet growing demands for computing capacity. And as expected, it captured Wall Street’s attention and became a headline throughout the AI industry.

Personally, what makes this interesting is that ChatGPT is considered the biggest threat to Alphabet’s search and ad business. It’s as if the two biggest rivals overlooked the competition and decided to work together to keep up with the demand.

With the OpenAI now working with Google Cloud, Alphabet essentially landed one of the most important clients in tech, and the numbers behind this market are massive - the global cloud AI is forecasted to grow more than 5x over the next few years: from ~$103 billion in 2025 to ~$590 billion in 2032 with a CAGR of 28.5%.

Also, the collaboration means that Alphabet’s custom-built TPUs (Tensor Processing Units) will finally be in the spotlight. TPUs are AI accelerators built to train AI models like Gemini, and they were historically intended for internal use only. But with OpenAI as their partner, ChatGPT will be using this technology moving forward, thus reducing OpenAI’s reliance on Microsoft.

In the best-case scenario, none of this appears to be baked into the stock price. More on that in a bit.

And it doesn’t end with OpenAI. Converting enemies to clients is something Alphabet appears to be doing more of.

Alphabet now has Apple, Anthropic, and OpenAI, in addition to OpenAI, using its infrastructure - three massive threats converted into high-paying clients.

The news broke out more than a week ago, but for some reason, the stock didn't appear to react positively to Alphabet. The share price was around $180, but it’s now hovering around $174.

Antitrust Matters

So, why isn’t the market more bullish on Alphabet? Alphabet’s Antitrust issues.

Recently, a federal judge ruled that Alphabet held illegal monopolies in online advertising. And this isn’t the first time that the company got into such a mess.

Back in August 2024, a D.C. federal judge also ruled that Alphabet violated antitrust laws by maintaining a monopoly in online ads, which was said to be the biggest antitrust ruling since Microsoft’s issues over 20 years ago.

As for the company’s recent lawsuit, the DOJ wants to prevent Alphabet from entering into those ‘exclusive deals’ such as making Alphabet the default search engine for Apple and Samsung phones.

Further, the DOJ also suggested Alphabet divest the Google Chrome browser as it’s considered responsible for creating an unfair advantage against competitors.

The latter is much more interesting considering that the browser is practically Google’s gateway in collecting user data that gets fed into its advertising platform - ad revenue currently makes up nearly 3 quarters of Alphabet’s top line.

Although the tech giant has yet to face the trial (scheduled for September 2025), some analysts are expecting a 25% decline in share prices should Alphabet be forced to sell Google Chrome. So, I suspect this to be the biggest reason Alphabet's catalysts aren't showing up (yet) in the stock price.

As I mentioned earlier, Alphabet was in a similar situation back in August 2024, and looking at the price chart shows that although the stock experienced a sell-off, it bounced back even stronger, except, it was then again hit with recent macroeconomic headwinds in Q1 ‘25, which was entirely irrelevant to the lawsuit.

Alphabet’s Financials

Despite the generally bearish stance on Alphabet, their financials have never looked so good.

Alphabet's revenue for the latest quarter increased by over 12% year-over-year to $90.2 billion, with net income up nearly 46% to $34.5 billion, or $2.81 per share basis (diluted).

It’s also worth noting that Alphabet has been outpacing market estimates over the past 4 quarters.

Zooming out, we see the annual figures aren’t much different. For FY’ 24, Alphabet’s annual revenue increased nearly 14% year-over-year to $350 billion, with the bottom line up around 36% to a cool $100 billion, or $8.04 per share (diluted).

Stretching 7 years back shows a more profound picture of the company’s financial trajectory:

As we can see from the chart, Alphabet’s financials have always been on an upward trend.

Alphabet’s segmental revenues paint the same picture:

Segments

Note: In Millions USD.

In terms of growth, the most impressive segment by far is Google Cloud. Its revenue for the quarter increased by over 28% to nearly $12.3 billion, thanks to enterprise adoption of cloud infrastructures and AI-powered services.

On the other hand, Alphabet’s core business segment "Google Services" grew 9.8% year-over-year, remarkably healthy for a business of this caliber. This is the segment where Alphabet generates ad revenues, which came in at $77 billion for the quarter alone.

Bottom line: Alphabet’s search advertising on the web and YouTube are still dominating in their respective markets.

Not only that, ad revenue continues growing despite the speculations around robots stealing organic traffic.

Google, however, is adapting to the competition.

Gemini itself is now answering most of the simple queries in the “AI Overview” section - and so far, AI Overview responses are excellent. Compelling enough not to use ChatGPT to search.

Bottom line: I'd be very worried if AI Overviews were not as good as they are - as LLMs like ChatGPT would have had a much easier chance of beating Google for search traffic.

Alphabet's Stock Price Action

In general, mature companies that regularly grow net income by 10, 20, or 30% a year have their stock price move at the same clip.

This story is no different.

Net income grew at an average compound growth rate (CAGR) of ~21.89%, and over the same period, the stock has grown at a CAGR of ~20.35%.

Looking closer, Alphabet’s rally had a nice run-up which peaked in late 2021 when the stock reached nearly $160. The surge was driven by the last wave of companies “going remote” during the pandemic that sent most tech stocks soaring. Remember the Zoom (ZM) craze?

Then, the sobering reality check came after 2022.

Google stock plummeted to around $90 per share.

And no, it wasn’t unique to Alphabet. The entire tech sector got hammered by the post-pandemic shifts as interest rates skyrocketed, making future earnings less valuable, and tech stocks like Alphabet had to bear the brunt of this repricing. Investors took a breather from growth stocks. In 2022, I was a net buyer of stocks.

A year later, many tech stocks began recovering, and as we can see today, Alphabet's stock has nearly doubled from its lowest points in 2022, thanks to the company’s predictable and solid business fundamentals. The bounce back has also been driven by factors like the company’s market position to capitalize on AI. Alphabet’s cloud computing segment also gained significant market share and, more notably, Alphabet’s core business has demonstrated resilience despite recession fears.

5-Year Price Return Comparison

Now I'll compare Alphabet against its competitors in the advertising and cloud computing space, which are Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), and Oracle (ORCL).

Meta crashed hard in 2022 while Oracle recently went parabolic, and although I’m bullish on both companies, Alphabet seems better at just chugging along - without any crazy swings or meltdowns, just performance.

Is Alphabet Undervalued?

I'm well aware that I'll get some flak for this, but, Alphabet is far more undervalued than many realize.

The company is printing truckloads of money on the back of its search engine, holding >93% of the market share, and as I explained earlier, I can’t immediately see that traffic going anywhere.

Moreover, the company’s been quietly building one of the most powerful AI infrastructures.

And with all that, out of all the companies mentioned above, only Alphabet trades at a lower multiple than its peer group, despite being one of the most sustainable and profitable companies.

At this point, buying a Ferrari at Honda prices is what perfectly describes Alphabet.

Wiz Acquisition

Apart from the OpenAI/Apple/Anthropic <-> Alphabet partnership, Alphabet just made another headline with its biggest bet ever. The tech giant is buying Wiz, the cloud security startup, for $32 billion.

Setting aside the fact that the global cybersecurity market is projected to reach over $500 billion by 2030, Alphabet is buying a rocket ship. Wiz became the fastest cybersecurity startup to reach $500 million in ARR. According to reports, Wiz is already serving 40% of the Fortune 100 companies. That’s impressive for a startup.

Interestingly, Wiz once turned its back on Alphabet’s $23 billion dollar offer last July. Wiz co-founder Assaf Rappaport wrote in a memo that "Saying no to such a humbling offer is tough." The reason for refusal was mainly because they were worried about the antitrust issues back then.

Has something changed?

I ask because… fast-forward to March 2025, Alphabet once again offered to buy the cloud security startup for $32 billion in an all-cash transaction - $9 billion more than the original offer. And guess what? Wiz accepted the deal with a termination fee of more than $3.2 billion. I guess Wiz must have either found a new comfort level with regulators, or the amount was too good to pass up.

What's more important is why would Alphabet value the company so much more than just eight months earlier?

If I were to guess, Alphabet is betting big that the acquisition will solve Google Cloud’s biggest challenges. According to industry analyst Gil Luria,

“For Google to be able to compete with Microsoft Azure for enterprise customers, it needs to be able to offer a deeper suite of services, including security software.”

Take note, according to recent stats, Google Cloud only holds about 12% of the market share. Amazon’s AWS has 30% while Microsoft has 21%, so Google Cloud is clearly the underdog.

By the looks of it, the acquisition of Wiz is Alphabet’s attempt to leapfrog the competition in cloud security, which is a critical area in AI and cloud computing infrastructures.

My Take

Unlike others like Intel (INTC), Alphabet didn't sit on their hands when it comes to AI - in fact, they’re constantly innovating and challenging the competition. But Alphabet’s antitrust suits are undoubtedly holding the stock back as the risks to the business are real. At the same time, the company is fighting to preserve its core business while simultaneously making massive investments like its Wiz acquisition.

Since 2018, Alphabet’s stock has risen almost exactly in line with its net income, making me believe the stock is fairly priced given the risks. However, should these risks resolve themselves (and I’m sure they will), this will be a story of regret. I believe investors will regret not having bought more Alphabet stock at today's prices because excitement will return. And excitement costs a lot.

My “Strong Buy” rating for Alphabet remains unchanged.

data.title: Cobalt Miners News For The Month Of June 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the June 2025 cobalt miners news.

The past month saw cobalt prices slightly lower.

Cobalt price news

As of June 18, the cobalt spot price was at US$15.12/lb, slightly lower from US$15.28/lb last month. The LME cobalt price is US$32,400/tonne. LME Cobalt inventory is 140 tonnes, no change from last month. More details on cobalt pricing (in particular the more relevant cobalt sulphate), can be found here at Benchmark Mineral Intelligence or Fast Markets MB.

Cobalt spot prices long-term price chart - USD 15.12 (source)

Cobalt demand vs. supply forecasts

S&P Global Market intelligence - Cobalt demand vs. supply forecast as of Oct. 2024 (source)

The global cobalt market balance is forecast to shift from surplus to deficit in 2027 (as of June 2024) (source)

Morgan Stanley forecasts cobalt surpluses to grow this decade (as of Nov. 2023) (source)

Trend Investing v IEA 2021 demand forecast for EV metals (Trend Investing) (IEA)

Cobalt market news

On May 19, Fastmarkets reported:

DRC prepares for new decision on cobalt export ban in June. The DRC is set to decide on the future of its cobalt export ban on June 22, potentially extending, modifying or ending the policy.

On May 27, Market Index reported:

Rise of the machines: How 1 billion humanoid robots by 2050 will drive a surge in critical minerals demand. Major broking and research firm Morgan Stanley have just released new modelling suggesting a massive increase in “humanoid” robots by 2050. This increase will inevitably require huge amounts of critical minerals, with rare earth minerals, lithium, copper, nickel, cobalt, and graphite at the forefront of booming demand...the potential big winners among these critical minerals becomes most apparent.

  • NdPr: Supply deficit of 13% by 2040; 26% by 2050

  • Lithium: Supply deficit of 75% by 2040; 78% by 2050

  • Copper: Supply deficit of 0.6% by 2040; 1.5% by 2050

  • Nickel: Supply deficit of 17% by 2040; 25% by 2050

  • Cobalt: Supply deficit of 16% by 2040; 34% by 2050

  • Graphite: not modelled...

On June 5, Reuters reported:

Metals investor Cobalt Holdings scraps London IPO plans. The company declined to specify the reasons for dropping its plans days after the listing was priced at $2.56 per share...Cobalt, which holds physical cobalt and has a contract to buy from Glencore, had plans to use the majority of the IPO proceeds to buy an initial 6,000 metric tons of the key battery metal, worth around $200 million, from the global miner.

On June 12, CSIS wrote:

Building a new market to counter Chinese mineral market manipulation... A major challenge to achieving mineral security is China’s manipulation of global markets, whereby Chinese companies flood the market with excess supply, driving prices down to levels that force mining operations in countries like the United States and Australia to shut down...In light of China’s aggressive use of export controls, building supply chains for critical minerals with allied nations is more crucial than ever. However, this cannot be done without market intervention, given China’s flooding of the market...the United States and its allies cannot afford to act in isolation. Unilateral efforts—whether through tariffs, subsidies, or investment restrictions—will remain insufficient given the relatively small market share of individual countries. Instead, building a unified anchor market that aligns the policies of like-minded nations is the only realistic path to confronting China’s dominance. By harmonizing tariffs, establishing collective quotas, and coordinating investment protections, the anchor market can shift leverage away from Beijing and toward a more resilient, rules-based minerals ecosystem.

On June 13, Todd Clewett (External Affairs Executive) announced on LinkedIn:

There's $2,500,000,000 in the 2025 Budget Reconciliation Bill for the US production of critical minerals through the National Defense Stockpile. There's a further $500,000,000 for the Dept of Defense Credit Program for loans, loan guarantees, and technical assistance for the development of reliable sources of critical minerals, both domestically and internationally. Congress is likely to pass the Bill (in some form) by end of July. This is going to happen fast.

On June 13, Miningdotcom reported:

Trump’s tax-cut bill could hold back US critical minerals projects. US President Donald Trump’s tax and spending bill would make it harder for American critical minerals companies to compete with China because it eliminates a tax credit for boosting domestic production of nickel, rare earths and other materials used in advanced electronics and weaponry...“One Big Beautiful Bill Act” last month that eliminates the so-called 45X credit. The Senate is now debating the bill.

Cobalt company news

CMOC Group Limited [HKSE:3993] [SHE:603993] (OTCPK:CMCLF) (formerly China Molybdenum)

On May 30, Market Screener reported: "Cmoc Group Limited announces Board and Committee changes, effective May 30, 2025."

On June 19, Market Screener reported: "Court grants final order to Lumina Gold, approving arrangement with CMOC."

Glencore [HK:805] [LSE:GLEN] (OTCPK:GLCNF)

On May 29, Cobalt Blue announced:

Cobalt Blue and Glencore contract to supply the Kwinana Cobalt Refinery...the execution of a contract with Glencore to provide cobalt hydroxide feedstock to the Kwinana Cobalt Refinery...

On June 16, Glencore announced: "Closing of Viterra/Bunge merger."

On June 17, Glencore announced: "Publication of 2024 Payments to Governments Report."

Zheijiang Huayou Cobalt [SHA:603799]

On June 11, Market Screener reported: "Huayou Cobalt issues 700 Million Yuan in sci-tech bonds; Shares up 3%."

Jinchuan Group International Resources [HK:2362]

On June 10, Jinchuan Group International Resources announced:

OPERATIONAL UPDATE FOR THE THREE MONTHS ENDED 31 MARCH 2025...In the First Quarter, due to the temporary suspension of the SX-EW system, the Group produced 71 tonnes of cobalt content included in cobalt hydroxide, representing a decrease of approximately 86.6% year-on-year as compared to that of 530 tonnes in the corresponding period in 2024...The Group did not sell any of cobalt as compared to that of 318 tonnes in the corresponding period in 2024 due to the low production and the export ban of the cobalt...Trading in the shares of the Company on the Stock Exchange has been suspended with effect from 9:00 a.m. on Friday, 28 March 2025 and will remain suspended until further notice.

Chemaf (subsidiary of Shalina Resources)

No news for the month.

GEM Co Ltd [SHE:002340]

On June 7, GEM Co announced:

...On June 5, 2025, to mark the 54th World Environment Day, GEM held a series of open-house events under the theme "Pioneering a Beautiful China, Shaping a Green Future" across its industrial parks...The activities offered the public a close-up look at GEM's recycling culture, promoted environmental knowledge and awareness, and showcased GEM's green industrial achievements.

Eurasian Resources Group (ERG) - private

ERG owns the Metalkol facility in the DRC where ERG processes cobalt and copper tailings with a capacity of up to 24,000 tonnes of cobalt pa.

On June 3, Mercur announced:

Eurasian Resources Group announces issuance of guaranteed bonds with an international credit rating in the amount of up to US$100 million...The bonds listed on the KASE are nominated in US dollars. In total, the offer comprises up to 100,000 bonds with a nominal value of US$1,000 per note and semi-annual coupon payments, a maturity of three years. The principal will be repaid in a single payment at the end of the term.

Umicore SA [Brussels:UMI] (OTCPK:UMICY)

On June 4, Umicore announced: "Average blood lead levels drop further near Umicore site in Hoboken."

Note: The Hoboken recycling facility recovers precious metals from recycled industrial by-products.

Sumitomo Metal Mining Co. (TYO:5713) (OTCPK:STMNF)(SMM)

On June 2, SMM announced:

Notice regarding the Status of Repurchase of Own Shares...Total number of shares repurchased 1,547,400 shares...From May 13, 2025 to May 31, 2025.

On June 19, SMM announced: "Updated Corporate Governance Report."

MMC Norilsk Nickel (Nornickel)

No news for the month.

Sherritt International [TSX:S] (OTCPK:SHERF)

On June 9, Sherritt International announced:

Sherritt announces the retirement of its Chairman...Sir Richard Lapthorne...effective today due to personal reasons.

On June 11, Sherritt International announced:

Sherritt reports shareholder meeting results, Board appointments and Committee Updates...the appointments of Leon Binedell as Executive Chairman...Leon Binedell...also serves as President and CEO of the Corporation.

Nickel 28 [TSXV:NKL] [GR:3JC] (OTCPK:CONXF)

Nickel 28 holds an 8.56% JV interest in Ramu which is operated by the Metallurgical Corporation of China (MCC).

On June 2, Nickel 28 announced:

Increase in Ramu Mineral Resources and Mineral Reserves...Nickel 28 currently holds an 8.56% joint-venture interest in Ramu...when the Company has repaid the construction debt, the Company will have the option to purchase an additional 9.25% interest in Ramu at market value, which if exercised would take the Company’s interest to 20.55%...The effective date of resource and reserve estimation is December 31, 2024.

  • 10% increase in total reserves year-over-year.
  • 18% increase in Measured & Indicated resources and 146% increase in Inferred resources...

On June 19, Nickel 28 announced: "Nickel 28 files Fiscal Q1 2026 Financial Statements."

Electra Battery Materials [TSXV:ELBM] (ELBM) (Electra)

On June 5, Electra announced:

...completion of a feasibility level Class 3 Engineering Study for the construction of a modular battery recycling facility adjacent to its cobalt sulfate refinery north of Toronto...The next phase of work, funded in part by Natural Resources Canada, will involve operating the recycling process.

On June 12, Electra announced: "Electra and Three Fires Group advance Canada’s first indigenous-led battery recycling venture."

On June 19, Electra announced:

Electra commences early works to support restart of North America’s only cobalt refinery...north of Toronto...The work, budgeted at approximately C$750,000, is particularly focused on advancing the solvent extraction facility...“By focusing on key infrastructure, particularly in the SX area, we are ensuring the site is ready for a seamless ramp-up as soon as full funding is in place.”

Other smaller cobalt producers

  • Vale SA (VALE)
  • Zijin Mining Group [SSE:601899]
  • China Nonferrous Metal Mining Company (CNMC)
  • Dongfang International Mining
  • Jiangsu Cobalt
  • Nanjing Hanrui Cobalt
  • Pengxin International Mining Co
  • Zijin Mining Group [SSE:601899]
  • Wanbau

  • Jiana Energy
  • Sicomes

Possible mid-term producers (2027 onwards)

Sunrise Energy Metals Limited [ASX:SRL] (OTCQX:SREMF)

Sunrise Energy Metals has 132kt contained cobalt at their Sunrise Project. They also own the Syerston Scandium Project.

No significant news for the month.

Ardea Resources [ASX:ARL] (OTCPK:ARRRF)

In total, Ardea has 6.1Mt of contained nickel and 386,000t of contained cobalt at their KNP Project near Kalgoorlie in Western Australia. Ardea is also exploring for gold and nickel sulphide on their >5,100 km2 of 100% controlled tenements in the Eastern Goldfields region of Western Australia.

No news for the month; however a new company presentation was posted.

Cobalt Blue Holdings [ASX:COB] (OTCPK:CBBHF) - Plan to change name to Core Blue Minerals Limited

Cobalt Blue has 87kt of contained cobalt at their 100% owned Broken Hill Cobalt Project [BHCP] in NSW, Australia. LG International is an equity strategic partner. COB also has an earn-in agreement (earn in to 51% up to 75%) for the Halls Creek Project in WA (copper-lead-zinc-silver-gold). They are also developing the Kwinana Cobalt Refinery.

On May 29, Cobalt Blue announced:

Cobalt Blue and Glencore contract to supply the Kwinana Cobalt Refinery...the execution of a contract with Glencore to provide cobalt hydroxide feedstock to the Kwinana Cobalt Refinery...Under the terms of the contract, Glencore will supply COB up to 50% of KCR’s feedstock requirements for a period of three years, commencing from the start-up of commercial operations.

On June 11, Cobalt Blue announced: "Halls Creek Project Scoping Study delivers a near-term copper-zinc opportunity."

Australian Mines [ASX:AUZ] (OTCPK:AMSLF)

On June 4, Australian Mines announced:

Expression of Interest – Sconi HMS Bergbau offtake...it has received a formal Expression of Interest [EOI] from HMS Bergbau AG (HMS), a leading German commodities trading and logistics group, regarding potential offtake of Nickel, Cobalt, and Scandium from the Company’s 100%-owned Sconi Project in Queensland. HMS is a globally recognised commodities trading house specialising in the marketing and logistics of raw materials, dry bulk, and critical minerals...The EOI — and the potential progression to a binding offtake agreement1 — represents an opportunity for the Sconi Project to access funding under the German Government’s critical minerals strategy.

Havilah Resources [ASX:HAV] [GR:FWL] (OTCPK:HAVRF)

Havilah 100% owns the Mutooroo copper-cobalt project, about 60km west of Broken Hill in South Australia. They also have the nearby Kalkaroo copper-gold-cobalt Project, as well as a potentially large iron ore project at Grants. Havilah’s 100% owned Kalkaroo copper-gold-cobalt deposit contains JORC Mineral Resources of 1.1 million tonnes of copper, 3.1 million ounces of gold and 23,200 tonnes of cobalt.

On May 30, Havilah Resources announced:

Quarterly activities and cash flow report - period ended 30 April 2025. Significant Events for the Quarter.

  • Kalkaroo copper-gold-cobalt project (Kalkaroo): Havilah continues to advance potential strategic funding discussions given the strong interest in asset-level development opportunity that has been buoyed by several recent copper project transactions.
  • Mutooroo copper-cobalt-gold project (Mutooroo): Following positive study program results, Havilah and JX Advanced Metals Corporation (JXAM) are presently negotiating the terms of an agreement for future funding of a Mutooroo pre-feasibility study and an option to acquire a project equity interest.
  • Regional exploration was focused on access and permitting work to allow drilling programs to proceed at Johnson Dam, Croziers, Birksgate and Mingary Mine prospects during the coming months.
  • Koba Resources Limited reported multiple >1,000 ppm eU 3O 8 intercepts from initial wide-spaced drilling over approximately 4 km of strike at the Everest prospect, which lies immediately north of Boss Energy Ltd’s Jason deposit in the Yarramba palaeochannel.
  • Heavy Rare Earths Limited completed passive seismic surveys to map uranium-bearing structures at Lake Namba-Billeroo and Prospect Hill projects and a high resolution airborne magnetic-radiometric survey over the Radium Hill project area.
  • Havilah participated in the inaugural AMEC (Association of Mining and Exploration Companies) investor briefing in Adelaide, see presentation and video.
  • Interim Financial Report for the financial half-year ended 31 January 2025 was released.
  • Kalkaroo, with its 3.1 million ounce open pit gold resource, is well positioned to capitalise on an escalating gold price, that surpassed US$3,500/oz ($5,400/oz) during April 2025 (see JORC table below for classifications/grades of Kalkaroo Mineral Resource).

Chilean Cobalt Corp. (C3) is a critical minerals exploration and development company focused on the La Cobaltera Project located in Chile’s historic San Juan cobalt district.

On June 4, Chilean Cobalt announced:

Extension of USD $317 Million Letter of Interest from US Exim Bank...The contemplated funding package would cover a majority of the anticipated construction and development capital required for Phase 1 of the La Cobaltera project, which targets the cobalt-copper oxide zones...While Chilean Cobalt has made good progress over the past 12 months, depressed and volatile cobalt prices have forced the Company to take a more cautious development approach. Looking ahead, cobalt market dynamics are shifting, and Chilean Cobalt anticipates submitting a formal application to EXIM during the next 12 months. Upon submission, EXIM will proceed with its final due diligence and underwriting process to evaluate a potential funding commitment. Concurrently, Chilean Cobalt continues to pursue additional US government support and strategic financing, including EXIM's new Supply Chain Resiliency Initiative (SRCI).

Conclusion

June saw cobalt spot prices slightly lower over the past month.

Highlights for the month were:

  • DRC prepares for a new decision on cobalt export ban in June, potentially extending, modifying or ending the policy.
  • Morgan Stanley forecasts a cobalt supply deficit of 16% by 2040; 34% by 2050.
  • Metals investor Cobalt Holdings scraps London IPO plans.
  • The West needs to build a new market to counter Chinese mineral market manipulation.
  • There's $2.5B in the 2025 Budget Reconciliation Bill for the US production of critical minerals through the National Defense Stockpile. There's a further $0.5B for the Dept of Defense Credit Program.
  • Trump’s tax-cut bill could hold back US critical minerals projects.
  • Huayou Cobalt issues 700 Million Yuan in sci-tech bonds.
  • Jinchuan Group International Resources produced 71 tonnes of cobalt content included in cobalt hydroxide in Q1, a decrease of ~86.6% YoY, zero sales due to the DRC cobalt sales ban.
  • ERG announces issuance of guaranteed bonds with an international credit rating in the amount of up to US$100 million.
  • Nickel 28 announces an 18% increase in Measured & Indicated resources and 146% increase in Inferred resources at Ramu.
  • Electra commences early works to support the restart of North America’s only cobalt refinery.
  • Cobalt Blue and Glencore contract to supply the Kwinana Cobalt Refinery.
  • Australian Mines receives an Expression of Interest for Sconi offtake from HMS Bergbau AG, a leading German commodities trading and logistics group.
  • TMC - Korea Zinc will make a strategic equity investment in TMC of $85.2M in exchange for 19.6M common shares at $4.34 per share.
  • Chilean Cobalt announces extension of USD $317M Letter of Interest from US Exim Bank.

As usual, all comments are welcome.

data.title: Conrad Industries: Marine Vessel Builder Gaining Traction Through Its Growing Backlog
data.description:
Intro

Conrad Industries, Inc. (OTCPK:CNRD) operates in the shipbuilding industry in the US, with the likes of steel-based harbor tugs & specialty barges being some of the popular finished products that the company delivers. The company makes vessels both for the private & public sectors in the US. Conrad also repairs & converts vessels, as we will learn from its revenue numbers in its first quarter earnings report this year. The Louisiana-based outfit has now 75 years of shipbuilding experience under its belt and currently trades with a market cap of approximately $116 million. The company has been built on values such as solid craftsmanship, top-notch service to customers, and above all, integrity.

As we see from the technicals, shares have rallied more than $4 a share or 24% since the release of the company's first quarter earnings report on the 15th of May this year. This has resulted in shares fastly approaching multi-year overhead resistance (Particularly Conrad's 2007 & 2022 highs). Being able to break through this resistance level is essentially where the opportunity lies in this stock. The reason being is that there is very little resistance above the $17 to $18 level if indeed shares manage to break through.

Suffice it to say, given the violent rally we have seen in Conrad over the past 5 weeks, there is definitely opportunity in this play if trading conditions persist for the remainder of fiscal 2025.

Q1 Earnings Report

Total revenues in the first quarter amounted to $80.5 million, generating a net profit of $3.9 million for the quarter, easily beating the $1.5 million comparable in the same period of 12 months prior. This means trailing GAAP income amounts to $13.58 million, resulting in a trailing P/E ratio of 8.56. Operating income over the same 12-month period amounts to $15.03 million, resulting in a trailing price to EBIT ratio of 7.73.

This is an excellent start, given the inverse of the company's trailing P/E ratio (Trailing earnings yield of 11.68%) immediately informs us of what shareholders would earn if management decided to pay out all of its GAAP profits in the form of a dividend.

Revenue Growth To The Fore

The key to Conrad's boost in profitability in Q1 was down to its revenue growth, which spiked by $14.2 million or 17.6% in the first quarter. Due to high fixed costs in this industry (gross margin came in at 8.73% in Q1), Conrad has to be in a position to be able to turn over its capital at speed in order to boost its return on capital numbers. it did this in the first quarter of this year.

Essentially, investing in Conrad is a play on whether one believes forward-looking revenue growth rates can surpass rising costs, which are seen across the board. Not only has management had to deal with rising steel prices, but tariffs have placed an extra layer of uncertainty concerning Conrad's forward-looking build costs. Furthermore, given how inflation has been impacting wages in recent times, management has also had to increase pay to employees to remain competitive in a fast-changing labour market.

Suffice it to say, rising costs must be offset by economies of scale both from a new-build standpoint as well as in its repairs segment.

We state this because any slowdown in revenue growth will have negative consequences right down the income statement, affecting net profit margin as a result. From a balance sheet standpoint, we see that Conrad's trailing return on equity comes in at 15.93%, easily outperforming the company's cost of equity (demonstrating value is being created). Furthermore, the level of cash on the balance sheet ($35.89 million) and minimal debt demonstrate the strong efficiency of the company's 5 ship-building yards. These trends are encouraging, especially if accounts receivable can continue to be reined in at a faster clip than revenue growth over time.

Enduring Competitive Advantages Leading To Growing Backlog

Conrad's backlog now comes in at a very strong $292 million, highlighting competitive advantages Conrad has at its disposal, as we learn below from comments on the Q1 earnings report for fiscal 2025.

Although we face substantial uncertainties in our markets, we believe we enjoy many competitive advantages, including our shipyard capacity, our investments in improving our shipyards’ capabilities and efficiencies, our experienced management and engineering teams, and our many devoted employees. We also believe that we benefit from a reputation for producing quality products and for workplace safety, as evidenced by being awarded the 2023 and 2024 American Equity Underwriters, Inc. (AEU) Safety Award and both the 2023 and 2024 Shipbuilders Council of America (SCA) “Excellence in Safety” awards and the 2023 “Improvement in Safety” award.

Forward-Looking Risks

Being a micro-cap play brings its own set of risks, which we outline below. Firstly, the company trades with below-average trading volume, which means aggressive selling could easily push the stock below key support levels. Micro-cap plays accordingly have to be actively managed to ensure one's position is always aligned with the prevailing trend.

Although Conrad has been able to attain multiple competitive advantages in recent decades, high fixed-cost businesses are often at the mercy of economic conditions, adversely affecting commodity prices as a result. A rising backlog may demonstrate growing demand, but the macro-picture invariably tends to decide how fast these ships can be delivered.

Potential slower delivery speeds affect how fast management collects its debts, which is a crucial aspect of being able to boost the company's return on capital over time. Therefore, while we acknowledge Conrad's potential, the company's low profit margins demand that revenue growth must remain strong, so that consistent shareholder value can be created over time.

Conclusion

To sum up, we are stamping a 'Hold' rating on Conrad Industries for the moment, although we may upgrade the stock to a 'Buy ' if we see considerable overhead technical resistance being surmounted with ease. The company's rising backlog, growing profitability, strong balance sheet, and cheap earnings point to strong potential if the right trading conditions stack up for the shipbuilder. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Kroger's Earnings And Why Private Labels Are Especially Appetizing Right Now
data.description:
Thesis: Amid signs of softening consumer spending, I believe value-oriented chains, like Kroger, are well-positioned to maintain steady performance. Kroger’s private labels, in particular, could do well in the months ahead by helping consumers save money (while bolstering KR’s bottom line).

I’ll dig into the details below, but we’re seeing enough signs that consumers are tightening their belts that it’s time to at least consider defensive positions. Kroger’s (NYSE:KR) P/E ratio has risen in recent months, but remains reasonable, especially in a market where high P/E ratios are becoming the norm. Kroger's heavy utilization of private labels, which are typically cheaper than name brands, could prove especially important as consumers shift into value mode.

Kroger's Earnings Recap and Review

Kroger just released its earnings, and overall, the numbers were solid, demonstrating steady growth and success. Zack's consensus estimate pegged EPS at $1.45, but Kroger's adjusted EPS came in slightly higher at $1.49 (Zack's estimate seems to be based on adjusted EPS). Identical sales excluding food were up 3.2%. Revenues, however, fell a bit short of Zach's consensus at $45.1 billion, vs an estimate of $45.38 billion.

E-commerce sales were up 15%, and identical sales grew by 3.2% from a year prior. The operating profit rose from $1.294 billion in Q1 2024 to $1.322 billion in Q1 2025. The declared dividend per common share weighed in at .32 cents, up from .29 cents a year prior.

All in all, KR's quarterly performance strikes me as steady and solid, although certainly not flashy. And that's what I expect from the stock's performance. As for the future, I believe that Kroger's popular store brands, including Private Selection and Simple Truth, make the company an especially attractive investment.

We’re Seeing Clear Signs Consumers Are Tightening Their Belts

I’ve already mentioned in previous articles that evidence is emerging that consumers are tightening their belts. McDonald’s (NYSE:MCD) has seen sales decline after years of steep price increases. Brinker International’s (NYSE:EAT) Chili’s chain is booming with sales driven by their Three-for-Me promo, and Yum!'s (NYSE:YUM) Taco Bell is enjoying success after rolling out a generous customer loyalty program. We’ve seen rising delinquency with credit cards and car loans (for now, primarily in the subprime segment), and credit usage overall is expanding.

New evidence continues to mount. Domino’s and Papa John’s are reporting a decline in delivery orders as customers pick up food instead, likely in an effort to save money. Alter Agents conducted a study and found that many people claim they are now cutting unnecessary spending, reducing eating out, and even canceling or postponing vacations. Meanwhile, loyalty programs, cash back, and the like are becoming more popular.

The sky isn't falling, to be clear. The changes in many cases are subtle, and perceptions could be as important in driving these changes as serious underlying developments. Wages are still rising, unemployment is holding steady, and progress is being made in trade talks.

Still, given all of the above, value-oriented brands may do well, while more expensive businesses may struggle. Upmarket luxury brands may continue to perform well as the luxury market is an entirely different segment, and rising asset prices could be pumping up the holdings of wealthy households. Yet when it comes to the middle and working classes, companies stuck in the middle, like McDonald’s, could be vulnerable.

Kroger is well-positioned in the current market, by my estimation. When people eat out less, they tend to shop at stores more to prepare food at home. When money is tight, people often shift to sales and coupon shopping, which KR can take advantage of. I’ll take a longer look shortly, but with all the above in mind, Kroger’s private-label brands could prove especially popular in the months ahead.

Conditions Could Worsen (But There is No Guarantee)

It’s possible that changing consumer behavior will turn out to be short-term. The trade war that kicked into high gear on “Liberation Day” rattled consumer confidence. If the economy holds strong and employment remains high, consumers may get past trepidations and go back to more normal spending habits.

There are a few concerning points, however. First, college graduates, especially Computer Science engineers, are suffering higher-than-normal unemployment. The Computer Science angle is especially interesting because it could hint at headwinds caused by Artificial Intelligence (but this is speculative, for now).

Meanwhile, the Ludwig Institute for Shared Economic Prosperity (LISEP) recently claimed that the “true unemployment rate” is actually 24.3%. This claim is based on the idea that many people are employed but are struggling to make ends meet. The U-6 unemployment rate, which includes people marginally attached to the workforce, is rather high.

Meanwhile, the labor force participation rate also remains historically low. Fewer people may be unemployed compared to the past, but fewer people are participating in the workforce at all.

I can’t say for certain that the economy is deteriorating. The trends noted above could prove to be a temporary response to the trade war, and businesses are in a “no fire, no hire” mode, which helps explain why college grads are struggling to find jobs. Yet, if the economy remains strong, hiring could pick up again. Still, there are enough concerning trends that make Kroger all the more appealing.

Kroger’s Private Labels Offer Excellent Value

One of my favorite parts about KR, both as a customer and investor, is their private labels. Private labels are designed as substitutes for more expensive name brands. While a name brand company will often have to spend extensive amounts on advertising to lure in customers, private labels typically utilize minimal advertising and instead try to first piggyback off name brands and then lure in customers with more affordable prices. Kroger has previously reported that over 90% of shoppers at their stores have purchased private-label products.

Walmart’s (NYSE:WMT) private labels, in my experience, are a bit iffy, whereas many Private Selection items are good enough that I’d actually take them over name brands even if they are at or nearly at the same price point. (Private Selection premium ice cream > Ben & Jerry’s, especially when price is factored in). Kroger’s Simple Truth brand also offers organics and other upmarket items reminiscent of Whole Foods and Sprouts.

Unsurprisingly, Kroger is one of the most successful private label companies, with generic sales making up more than 22% of revenue. Walmart is slightly ahead, and at the right valuation, would be an excellent choice in the face of inflation, but its current valuation is simply too high for me. Aldi tops the chart, and I’d love the chance to invest in them at a decent evaluation, but it’s a privately held company.

Not only do private labels save customers money, but they actually produce higher profits for grocery stores than name brands. Whereas a name brand is typically marked up 26%, a private label usually offers margins of 35% on average. If inflation picks up, I expect more and more consumers to turn to private labels. Even if inflation remains tempered, consumers are already under enough pressure to look for bargains.

In Kroger’s case, this could turn out to be a major benefit not just right now but going forward. With the right high-quality private label items, the company may be able to build a larger, durable customer base that could stick around even after the economic environment improves. (This is actually how I became a Private Selection/Simple Truth fan. Some tough times more than a decade ago had me tightening my own belt.)

It should also be noted that the high-profit margin enjoyed by private labels will make it more affordable to launch promotions. Kroger also likely won’t need to coordinate with external parties (at least not as extensively) either.

Kroger Maintained Net Income and Revenues During the Great Recession

One of the most appealing things about Kroger is its performance during the Great Recession. While many companies struggled, Kroger soldiered on with revenues gradually rising. The past doesn’t guarantee the future, but I suspect KR will continue to outperform should a recession set in. Share prices could still take a hit and margins could come under pressure, but that might make KR an even more attractive buy.

The chart below shows KR making slow but steady revenue gains even amid the Great Recession. I included Walmart, another value king, to demonstrate that it’s likely not a fluke but more likely part of a general trend.

Kroger Fundamentals and Valuation

Kroger’s P/E ratio is a bit of a concern and is historically rather high, although it has at times been higher in the past. Their GAAP P/E (TTM) is at ~18, compared to 14 at the end of 2022 and below 10 at the end of 2020. Still, the P/E ratio isn’t outside of historical ranges, as the chart below shows.

The P/E ratio is much lower than Walmart’s P/E ratio. Walmart is also a value king, at least as far as store shelf prices are concerned. However, it’s not a value king when looking at P/E ratios, as illustrated below. Some other companies, like Target (NYSE:TGT), have a substantially lower P/E ratio, although I believe Target’s lack of emphasis on groceries could hurt them a bit during a recession.

Gurufocus’s DCF calculator, which I use to compute reverse DCF calculations to forge a baseline to justify current share prices, suggests that Kroger isn’t overvalued. The first calculation uses an EPS measurement (with the discount rate set at 10% per standard, and the terminal growth set at 4%, in line with typically observed global GDP rates). To justify current values, KR needs to maintain EPS growth rates of just 5.78%. When switching to free cash flows (same assumptions), KR needs to maintain a 14.56% growth rate, which, while rather high, is probably doable in the long term.

Seeking Alpha’s quant tools give KR a hold, which in and of the stock itself seems like a fair assessment. However, I support a buy rating owing to the general economic environment and KR’s past performance during hard times. Valuation takes a ding with a C+ rating. Growth is worse with a D+ rating, but since Kroger is limited mostly to the Midwest and South footprint-wise, I believe the company could spur growth with regional expansions.

Kroger owns CityMarket, which operates in flyover states, but the chain has only 34 stores. Kroger also owns Fred Meyer (132 stores), which is centered in Oregon and Washington. The brand operates a number of smaller chains as well, but I believe there’s still room for growth.

Revenue growth, unfortunately, is in the red at -1.94%. While concerning, it’s not enough to turn me away from KR, although I’ll be keeping a close eye on revenue going forward. Forward revenue growth is set at a modest .79%, which, if achieved, would satisfy me for now. The net income margin is only 1.81%. These numbers are a bit lacking, but if consumers do shift into value mode, I believe Kroger can raise the bar.

Take-Away: KR Could Post Steady Long-Term Gains and Provide a Near-Term Haven

Value is king in today’s economy. Even if the turbulence proves to be short-lived and consumer spending and confidence rise further down the road, through at least the next several weeks, I suspect consumers will be careful with their wallets. That makes consumer staples and grocery stores all the more appealing.

Kroger is especially appetizing owing to its private labels, which combine consumer affordability and generally high margins, something I often don’t see these days. Kroger isn’t a flashy stock, and massive upswings are unlikely. Yet, sharp downswings are unlikely as well. While I view KR as a safe option through the near future, I believe it can be a long-haul workhorse stock as well. This was reflected in Kroger's 2025 Q1 earnings report, which showed steady growth overall. Expanding e-commerce sales, by the way, might be able to propel KR beyond my positive expectations in the future.

data.title: FedEx Earnings Preview: Valuation Getting Compelling, But Negative Revisions Continue
data.description:
FedEx (NYSE:FDX), the freight and transport giant, reports their fiscal Q4 ’25 after the closing bell, next Tuesday, June 24th.

The stock is down 20% YTD in ’25 and down -7.77% in the last twelve months after guiding lower for fiscal Q4 ’25 in March ’25, and has now guided the last 3 quarters lower, despite the focus on reducing FedEx’s cost structure by $2 billion.

After becoming positive on FedEx’s prospects after management initiated the cost reduction and FedEx One (consolidation of operations), in early 2023, the earnings release in December ’23 was not a positive and decided to exit the stock. (The problem with FDX as this blog has written many times about the freight giant, which is also true of many industrial companies, is a small slowdown in revenue can cause much bigger changes in EPS thanks to the operating leverage.)

Even with the Freight spinoff, which is due to get completed sometime in fiscal ’26, the stock hasn’t been able to generate any upside.

On Tuesday night, June 24th, 2025, analyst consensus is expecting FedEx to print $5.87 in earnings per share, on $21.8 billion in revenue, and $1.95 billion in operating income, for expected year-over-year (y-o-y) growth of +9%, -1% and +4% respectively.

Eight of the last 11 FedEx quarters have seen negative y-o-y revenue growth. If FedEx meets the revenue estimate Tuesday night, FDX revenue for fiscal ’25 will have been flat, with just 2% expected for fiscal ’26.

Fiscal ’26 guidance today is expecting $19.75 in earnings per share on $87.5 billion in revenue for expected y-o-y growth of +9% and +2% respectively.

The onset of the pandemic in March, 2020, resulted in a huge surge in volume and operating leverage across the FedEx system, only to see this slow in 2022 (and continue to slow) with the rate hikes.

Revenue growth rates (yoy):

  • 4-quarter avg: 0%
  • 12-quarter avg: -1%
  • 20-quarter avg: +6%
  • 40-quarter avg: +7%

Operating income growth (yoy):

  • 4-quarter avg: -2%
  • 12-quarter avg: -2%
  • 20-quarter avg: +19%
  • 40-quarter avg: +9%

Valuation:

FedEx stock is about 10% -15% cheap to perceived intrinsic value (probably not enough of a discount to attract long-term investors given the operating leverage involved at FedEx), at its current price around $223 per share, trading at 12x earnings for expected EPS growth in fiscal ’26 of 9% on 2% revenue growth. The compelling valuation metrics for FDX are the price-to-TTM revenue value of 0.6x, and the price-to-TTM cash-flow metric of 7x. (TTM is trailing twelve month.)

The 6% free-cash-flow yield looks compelling as well.

Trends in EPS and revenue revisions:

Trends in revenue revisions:

Neither of these EPS and revenue revisions portend well for FedEx. The revisions remain negative, and the revenue revisions are more worrisome than the EPS revisions.

The chart:

Summary/conclusion

Looking at XPO Logistics (XPO) and UPS (UPS) stock, both well off their all-time-highs (and UPS is looking far worse than XPO or FedEx,) it’s clear that FedEx’s issues are not all company-related. Increased ground competition from companies like Shein and others, following in Amazon’s (AMZN) footsteps with a proprietary home delivery service, I’ve wondered now these services impact FedEx Ground. (UPS peaked at over $233 per share in early ’22, but is now trading under $100 per share. Haven’t followed the stock fundamentally since the 1990s. No opinion on its investment merits, and never followed XPO either, just looking at charts.)

The negative EPS and revenue revisions are the biggest issue around FedEx right now given the tariff kerfuffle, the sharply higher crude oil price (jet fuel for FedEx), but FedEx can put on a fuel surcharge to protect itself from higher crude prices.

Technically, the upward-sloping trendline for FedEx stock off the Covid March ’20 lows is around the $200 share price (see above chart) or roughly 10% lower than where the stock is trading today.

Clients have no position in the stock other than one longer-term, tax sensitive client that has a FedEx position purchased in March, 2000. The key question for readers though is would this blog commit to a decent weighting in the stock if FDX should trade below $190 – $200 over the next few quarters, and I’m sure that will happen. (Will try and answer that at a later date.)

None of this should be construed as advice or a recommendation but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. The information above may or may not be updated, and if updated may not be done in a timely fashion.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

data.title: Ryanair: Robust Summer Demand Combined With Pricing Power, Buy Confirmed
data.description:
Our airline coverage has proven solid — following a successful call on International Airlines Group, we’ve recently upgraded Lufthansa Group to a strong buy. We're also pleased with the performance of our Ryanair (NASDAQ:RYAAY, OTCPK:RYAOF) coverage (Fig. 1), which continues to validate our constructive view on the sector. As a reminder, our buy rating on Ryanair was supported by 1) robust traffic trends with A Decade of Growth Ahead, 2) higher earnings estimates that remain ahead of consensus (thanks also to its hedging strategy), and 3) its ultra-low-cost profile underpins our expectation of further market share gains.

With the company having reached our target price and just released its earnings, this is an appropriate moment to refresh our twelve-month outlook.

Fig 1

FY Results and Our Positive View

We note positively Ryanair’s record of carrying 200 million passengers in a single year. In 2025, the company's average fare decreased by 7%, accompanied by a 1% increase in revenue from ancillary sales. Q4 total unit costs were modestly up, with an additional note on the split between fuel and ex-fuel costs. In numbers, sales reached €13.95 billion (by 4% on a yearly basis) with operating expenses rising by 9% to €12.39 billion. Compared to last year, the company's operating profit decreased by 24%. However, upon examining the P&L, Ryanair reported a positive net interest due to a strong cash balance sheet. In March, cash almost reached €4 billion despite €1.6 billion in CAPEX and a complete €1.5 billion share buyback.

Source: Ryanair FY results ppt - Fig 2

Fig 3

Why are we Positive?

  1. Recent geopolitical events in the Middle East are likely to impact the airline sector. There are at least three main downsides: 1) lower traffic growth with impact on direct exposure; however, Ryanair has limited impact on the area, and we also highlight Wizz as having the most significant exposure (among the EU low-cost carriers), 2) lower demand mainly due to negative GDP impact, and 3) oil impact on the airlines P&L (even in this regards, is very well protected compared to the EU industry). In terms of numbers, the company has hedged its oil price at 78% and 84% in 2025 and 2026, respectively (Fig. 5). The aggregate EU airlines have hedged in the 75% area for the current financial year. This provides solid downside protection;

  2. On a positive note, Ryanair will benefit from the Easter shift in Q1 2026 (its calendarized year). During the Q&A analysts call, the company stated that peak summer fares are trending modestly ahead year-over-year, with 30% already booked. Additionally, Ryanair pricing is tracking in the mid-to-high teens, and we see fares even higher due to the high prices from air carriers' flags (and the EU sector consolidation). Aside from easier comparatives, the company restored its OTA network, which might provide volume growth ahead;

  3. Aligned with our supportive equity story in the Lufthansa MRO division, a tight industry supply environment will result in no capacity additions in 2026, and this should aid upward pressure on ticket prices, with Ryanair the first beneficiary;

  4. While some may take issue with the CEO’s remarks, his track record in creating shareholder value remains strong. The company announced a new €750 million share buyback (Fig. 4), which represents 3% of its entire market capitalisation (this came on top of the 7% of shares bought back and already cancelled). This new buyback is likely to run for the next 6-12 months. Top management noted that the buyback could pause in 2026 and 2027 due to bond maturities and CAPEX needs. As a reminder, we note that if the company's shares trade above €21 for 28 consecutive days, the CEO is entitled to 10 million share options to be exercisable at an €11 strike price. That said, the shares will be exercisable in 2028, provided he remains the CEO. We appreciate the precise alignment of interest this creates between the CEO and shareholders.

Fig 4

Fig 5

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Following the release of our full-year 2025 results, we have updated our financial forecasts at the Lab. Ryanair’s guidance came in ahead of our expectations, albeit echoing themes already shared by other travel sector names. Additionally, our team had not anticipated the announcement of a €750 million share buyback, particularly in light of the upcoming bond redemption and macroeconomic uncertainties surrounding travel demand.

That said, we’ve now revised our estimates to reflect the buyback, fuel and FX hedging, as well as updated assumptions for ex-fuel cost inflation and fare trends. Based on the management comment, our fare projections now incorporate quarterly growth assumptions of +15.5%, +2%, –3.5%, and –1% for Q1 through Q4, respectively, resulting in an average increase of roughly 3.75%. This resulted in our sales estimates reaching €15.7 billion. On the cost side, our fuel-adjusted modeling now assumes the ex-fuel cost per passenger will rise approximately 4%, compared to our prior estimate of +2.5%. While we see upside potential to pricing, weaker demand could still lead to fare-driven stimulus. Our EBITDAR reached €3.7 billion, and considering the lower interest income resulting from the buyback, we arrived at a net income of €2.25 billion. Considering the company’s share repurchase and the reduction in shares outstanding from 1.06 billion to 1.028 billion, our EPS increased to 2.18. Overall, these revisions lift our EPS estimates for 2026 by 1.5%.

Given the current fundamentals, we see limited justification for valuing the company at a 20% discount to post-COVID levels; instead, we apply a 12x P/E and 7x EV/EBITDA target as a more balanced reflection of its earnings power. Therefore, we derive a target price of €26.5 per share ($60.8 in ADR).

Risks

Given our in-depth coverage of Ryanair, we highlight several key risks: 1) intensifying competition from low cost operators such as EasyJet and Wizz Air, 2) ongoing aircraft delivery delays that may lead to mismatches in staffing costs (even if might increase the company’s fare price), 3) change in oil price, 4) weakening consumer demand, which particularly affects price-sensitive related to leisure travel, 5) event risks tied to natural disasters, terrorism, and wars (such as the Middle East and Ukraine-Russia conflict), 6) cyberattacks risks, as seen recently with CrowdStrike’s software outage, and 7) airport disruption such as the Heathrow fire. Additionally, in our estimates, we continue to value the company above Wall Street analysts' estimates, and we have removed its 20% discount on a P/E basis. This is primarily due to the company’s dominant market share in the EU and its ability to capture even more market share from weaker players over the coming years.

Conclusion

We continue to rate the company a buy, seeing further upside due to its lower oil sensitivity, a strong management team, and potential for higher sales resulting from industry capacity constraints. The next catalyst will be the Q1 2026 results, which are scheduled to be announced on July 21st.
